items.q: In which direction does an electric field point near a positive charge?
items.correct: Radially outwards — the field is defined by the force on a positive test charge, which is repelled.
items.misconception: Radially inwards because positive attracts negative.
items.random_wrong: There is no field around a positive charge unless another charge is present.
items.why: Students confuse the convention (positive test charge) and forget that an isolated positive source still has a field even without another charge.

items.q: If the distance between two masses doubles, what happens to the gravitational force between them?
items.correct: It quarters — gravitational force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
items.misconception: It halves — gravitational force is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It doubles — more distance means more space for gravity to act through.
items.why: Students default to linear relationships and forget the inverse-square law; they apply 1/r reasoning instead of 1/r^2.

items.q: What does the spacing of field lines on a diagram indicate?
items.correct: The strength of the field — closer lines mean a stronger field.
items.misconception: Field lines are equally important regardless of spacing — only the arrows matter.
items.random_wrong: Wider spacing means a stronger field because the lines have more room to act.
items.why: Students treat field lines as a count rather than a density-based representation; they miss that the convention encodes strength via spacing.

items.q: Units of electric field strength are:
items.why: Correct answer: V/m. “J/C” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Field lines point in the direction of force on a:
items.why: It's Positive test charge. “Negative charge” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Plate gap 0.02 m, potential 100 V. Field strength (V/m)?
items.why: The real figure is 5000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
